other_observers: Mark Peterson, Brandon Percival, Glenn Walbek, Loch Kilpatrick and another individual (sorry, can't remember name);
description: Mark Peterson, Brandon Percival, and I were joined by Glenn Walbek, Loch Kilpatrick and another individual (sorry, can't remember name) on the afternoon of 1 May. We were watching shorebirds in mid-afternoon at Lake Holbrook. The sun was to our backs, heat waves were not problematic with the closer shorebirds, which were 100 yards away or so. I was using a Kowa 88 mm scope with 20-60x eyepiece at 60x, comfortably.  Description After about 20 minutes, I noticed a dowitcher (there were about 125 dowitchers present), that had orange only from throat through chest (maybe uppermost belly). The base color of the remainder of the underparts was whitish. It also had rather prominent barring on the sides, turning into diffuse spotting on sides of chest. The orange color was paler than the deeper rufous of the surrounding LB Dowitchers. The orange color extended onto face. The upperparts appeared paler than that of the LB Dows, not with feather tips more contrasting, per se, but with an overall more patterned look. Plate 82.7 of Paulson's shorebird book gives a good representation, except that some of the gray wing coverts were retained from "winter" plumage. The primary projection was visible, whereas it was not in the nearby Long-billed Dowitchers (short projection). This could, however, have been due to incomplete growth of the tertials. Notably, however, the banding in the tertials were fresh, and they were more contrasty, with broader orange-ish bands, than in the LBDOs. The tail pattern was not clearly seen. The bill length was within range of the nearby Long-billeds, but when it held its bill up once, for a relatively prolonged period of time, a small kink (often, but not always visible in SBDO in my experience, occasionally seen in LBDO in my experience) was detectable. No obvious size difference was noted (between this bird and LBDOs), but the bird had a more "flat-backed" and a less "football like" shape when actively feeding.  While I was looking at this bird, Mark had found two other dowitchers of interest. These looked more like typical hendersoni and were in full alternate plumage as far as we could detect. Their underparts were not as deeply colored as the LBDOs, but the orange underparts did extend from throat/face to undertail coverts. The upperparts were very much like Plate 82.7 in Paulson's book. The difference in tertial markings between these birds and the LBDOs was noticeable, and similar to bird 1. The sides were distinctly barred breaking into light spotting on the sides of the chest. The primaries could be seen to project beyond the tertials (short projection). Tail pattern was, again, not quite visible. When feeding, the flatter backed appearance was consistently evident. Both birds looked shortish billed, one perhaps shorter than the range of LBDOs present. Neither of these two birds presented their bill in profile long enough to determine details of shape.   The facial pattern (crown, supercilium) was not scrutinized in any of these birds, excepting that the supercilium was rather broad anteriorly in all three, seemingly more so than most of the LBDOs present.   No vocalizations were heard.Behaviors: See descriptionCall: not heardPlumage: adult
